name: Musical
description: Stories where emotion becomes so intense and interior that spoken dialogue can no longer contain it, requiring characters to break into song and movement to express what ordinary language cannot. The musical form promises audiences a heightened emotional reality in which the world itself cooperates with feeling, and every number must earn its place by advancing the story or deepening our understanding of a character. (This graph has 18 nodes — one more than the 17-node standard — because it includes a second Anti-Pattern node to capture an additional genre-specific pitfall.)

nodes.label: Musical Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised a story where emotion is so intense it transcends spoken dialogue — where characters sing and move because the interior life demands a fuller outlet than words alone can provide. This promise establishes a heightened reality in which the audience agrees to accept song as a natural extension of feeling rather than an interruption of it. Critically, the promise is not merely spectacle: every number must justify itself by revealing something about character or advancing the story's stakes, or the contract with the audience breaks.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as a musical
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that will govern when and why characters sing
typical_variants: Stage-derived musical where theatrical convention governs the world
Film musical where cinematic grammar expands the scale of numbers
Sung-through form where music replaces dialogue almost entirely
Musical comedy where song amplifies humor and social observation
failure_modes: Promise of emotional intensity but songs that feel decorative rather than emotionally inevitable
Establishing a world where song feels arbitrary rather than earned by feeling
signals_in_text: Opening number immediately establishes the heightened emotional register
Characters shift from speech to song at moments of peak emotional pressure
The world treats singing as a natural mode of expression, not a spectacle

nodes.label: Songs Must Advance Plot or Reveal Character
nodes.definition: Every musical number must do narrative or dramatic work: it either moves the plot forward, deepens our understanding of a character's interior life, or changes the relationship between characters. A number that could be removed without consequence is a structural failure, not a highlight. This is the foundational discipline of integrated musical storytelling that separates the form from a play with songs inserted between scenes.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Each number is anchored to a specific dramatic function that cannot be served as effectively by spoken dialogue
typical_variants: Plot-advancing number that changes the situation or reveals new information
Character-revealing number that exposes desire, fear, or contradiction
Relationship number that shifts the dynamic between two characters
failure_modes: Songs that pause the narrative to entertain without revealing anything new
Numbers that repeat dramatically what dialogue has already established
Production numbers that showcase the ensemble with no narrative grounding
signals_in_text: The story's situation is demonstrably different after a number ends
Lyrics carry information or emotional revelation unavailable in prior dialogue
Characters' relationships or intentions are altered by the act of singing together

nodes.label: Emotional Escalation Justifies the Musical Break
nodes.definition: A character bursts into song because the emotion has reached a pitch that spoken language cannot contain — the transition from dialogue to song must feel inevitable, not arbitrary. This escalation principle means writers must engineer the scene leading into every number so that the audience feels the pressure building and experiences the song as release rather than interruption. When the escalation is missing, the musical break feels like a commercial or a variety act grafted onto drama.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
Scenes leading into numbers are structured to build emotional pressure
exit_conditions: The transition from speech to song at each number feels emotionally earned
typical_variants: Joy or desire reaching a pitch where speech cannot fully express it
Grief or longing so acute it demands a fuller outlet
Conflict between characters escalating until only confrontational song can hold it
failure_modes: Characters break into song without any emotional buildup
The scene preceding a number ends flatly, making the song feel pasted on
Characters return to speech after a number with no acknowledgment of the emotional peak just reached
signals_in_text: Dialogue accelerates and becomes more heightened just before a number
Stage directions or scene construction indicate a moment of emotional overload
The audience or reader feels the inevitability of the number before it begins

nodes.label: The World Must Establish When and Why Characters Sing
nodes.definition: Every musical must negotiate its own relationship between speech and song — whether characters are aware they are singing, whether song is diegetic or internal, whether the world is naturalistic or frankly theatrical. This negotiation must happen early and be held consistently, because the audience's suspension of disbelief depends on a stable contract. A musical that shifts its rules mid-story about who can sing, when, and why will lose the audience's trust in the most basic premise of the form.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: The story's internal logic for singing is established within the first act and maintained throughout
typical_variants: Theatrical convention: characters sing in a frankly artificial world that acknowledges performance
Emotional realism: characters sing as expressions of interiority while the external world continues
Diegetic integration: characters are musicians or performers for whom singing is professionally motivated
failure_modes: The rules about who sings and why shift inconsistently between numbers
Some characters sing freely while others never do, with no internal logic explaining the difference
The world treats one number as theatrical and the next as realistic with no tonal bridge
signals_in_text: Early numbers establish the mode — the audience learns from the first songs how singing works in this world
Supporting characters' relationship to song is consistent with the protagonist's
Musical logic is maintained even when the subject matter is dark or comedic

nodes.label: Musical and Narrative Structure Must Be Integrated
nodes.definition: The architecture of the musical — where acts break, where reprises fall, where the final number lands — must mirror and reinforce the story's dramatic structure, not run parallel to it. Act-break numbers must heighten the central dramatic question; reprises must carry transformed emotional meaning from their originals; the final number must resolve or crystallize what the story has been building. When musical structure and narrative structure drift apart, the form fragments into separate entertainment and story.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
Story structure is being designed alongside musical structure
exit_conditions: The placement and content of numbers map onto the story's key dramatic beats
typical_variants: Classic book musical structure: overture, act one establishing numbers, act-break showstopper, act two resolution
Sung-through structure: dramatic arc carried entirely by music without spoken breaks
Concept musical structure: thematic numbers rather than linear plot numbers
failure_modes: Numbers clustered at the start then absent for long stretches, making the story feel like a play with opening songs
Act break arrives at a dramatically arbitrary moment because a big number happens to fall there
Reprises placed by musical convention without regard for whether the emotional moment warrants return
signals_in_text: Dramatic turning points coincide with major numbers rather than falling between them
Act breaks land on a musical moment that crystallizes the central question
The song list, read in sequence, tells the emotional story of the show

nodes.label: Classic Book Musical
nodes.definition: The integrated musical play: a through-line narrative with defined characters, spoken dialogue scenes alternating with numbers, and a dramatic arc that depends on both song and book scenes to function. The book musical is built on the principle that the spoken scenes and the sung scenes should be indistinguishable in their dramatic seriousness — neither is filler for the other. Story and character are the organizing principles, and the musical numbers are the moments where the story's emotional core breaks through the surface of ordinary speech.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Story has a clear protagonist, objective, and dramatic arc
exit_conditions: The story's resolution depends on what happens in both the book scenes and the musical numbers
typical_variants: Romantic book musical: story built around a central love relationship
Social book musical: story built around a community under pressure or transformation
Character-study book musical: protagonist's internal journey drives the arc
failure_modes: Book scenes feel like connective tissue between the real show, which is the songs
Characters established in song are contradicted by their behavior in spoken scenes
Story resolves entirely in dialogue with songs absent from the climax
signals_in_text: Dialogue scenes move the plot and build toward numbers rather than summarizing them
Characters' spoken and sung voices are continuous — same person, different register
The climax and resolution use both song and dialogue as equally essential tools

nodes.label: Jukebox Musical
nodes.definition: A musical built around pre-existing songs from a specific artist, band, or era, where the narrative is constructed to justify and emotionally inhabit those songs rather than commissioned for an original score. The central craft challenge of the jukebox musical is making the audience forget they know the songs — or better, making their prior knowledge deepen the emotional experience — by placing familiar music in dramatically charged contexts that reveal new dimensions in lyrics the audience may have heard hundreds of times. When this works, the form creates a double pleasure: recognition and discovery simultaneously.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Pre-existing song catalog is the musical foundation
exit_conditions: The narrative provides a dramatic container that makes the known songs feel specifically motivated in their placement
typical_variants: Biographical jukebox: story of the artist whose songs are used
Catalogue jukebox: fictional story constructed around a body of songs
Tribute jukebox: narrative frame for revisiting an era's music
failure_modes: Songs are placed in the order that makes biographical or chronological sense rather than dramatic sense
The story exists only as delivery mechanism for hit songs, with no dramatic stakes of its own
Lyrics don't match the dramatic context — the song's original meaning fights the scene's meaning
signals_in_text: Song placement is motivated by the character's dramatic need in the scene, not the song's original context
The narrative has genuine stakes independent of the audience's affection for the music
Familiar songs land with fresh emotional charge because of how the scene positions them

nodes.label: Rock Opera / Sung-Through Musical
nodes.definition: A musical form in which spoken dialogue is replaced entirely or nearly entirely by sung text, demanding that the music carry all the work of exposition, characterization, dramatic escalation, and emotional resolution that book musicals distribute between spoken scenes and songs. The sung-through form intensifies the integration requirement — there is no relief valve of ordinary speech, so every melodic phrase must serve the drama. The rock opera variant brings the energy, distortion, and expressive vocabulary of rock music to this total-immersion approach, often in service of elevated or mythological subject matter.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Narrative and character are carried entirely through music and lyric
exit_conditions: The story is legible and emotionally complete without any spoken dialogue
typical_variants: Classical sung-through: operatic vocal style applied to musical theater subject matter
Rock opera: rock musical vocabulary applied to grand or mythological narratives
Pop sung-through: contemporary pop idioms used to tell intimate character stories
failure_modes: Exposition is unclear because there is no spoken dialogue to clarify plot logistics
Emotional peaks are indistinguishable because the entire form operates at elevated intensity
Recitative passages connecting dramatic numbers feel dramatically inert
signals_in_text: No spoken dialogue — all text is lyric or sung recitative
Musical motifs carry dramatic meaning that would be carried by spoken exposition in a book show
Emotional climaxes are built through musical escalation rather than dialogue confrontation

nodes.label: Musical Comedy
nodes.definition: A musical in which comedy is the primary mode and the musical numbers amplify, accelerate, and crystallize the comedy rather than interrupting it. Musical comedy depends on a specific tonal contract: the audience knows bad things are unlikely to end badly, and the heightened emotional reality of the form is deployed in service of wit, physical absurdity, social satire, and romantic confusion. Songs in musical comedy often work by the comic principle of the unexpected — rhymes that surprise, emotions that seem disproportionate, situations revealed to be more complicated than anyone admitted.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Primary mode is comedic — situations escalate absurdly, resolution is happy or at least satisfying
exit_conditions: Comic situations are resolved, romantic complications untangled, and the tone lands as celebratory
typical_variants: Farce-inflected musical comedy: plot driven by mistaken identity and escalating confusion
Satirical musical comedy: comedy emerges from exposing the absurdity of social institutions
Romantic musical comedy: comic obstacles delay a love story with a foregone but satisfying conclusion
failure_modes: Tone shifts to genuine darkness without earning the shift, breaking the comedic contract
Comedy is purely verbal without using the musical form's specific comic possibilities
Romantic resolution feels unearned because the comedy was too busy being funny to build the relationship
signals_in_text: Numbers that escalate situations beyond the bounds of plausibility in ways that delight rather than alarm
Lyrics deploy unexpected rhymes and comic timing that prose cannot achieve
The world's troubles are real but the audience is always confident they will be resolved

nodes.label: Heightened Emotional Reality
nodes.definition: The world of a musical operates at a register of emotional intensity slightly or significantly above naturalistic reality, where feelings are larger, desires more urgent, and the expression of interiority more immediate than in everyday life. This heightening is not a failure of realism but the genre's fundamental operating principle — it is the atmospheric condition that makes song feel natural rather than strange. The production design, staging, and prose style must all contribute to establishing and maintaining this heightened register, because if the world is too flat and naturalistic, singing will feel absurd rather than inevitable.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
World-building choices are being made
exit_conditions: The story's aesthetic register consistently supports song as a natural emotional outlet
typical_variants: Theatrical heightening: frankly artificial design that acknowledges the form's conventions
Cinematic heightening: realistic settings transformed by camera movement, choreography, and editing
Intimate heightening: small-scale world where emotional intensity is concentrated rather than expanded
failure_modes: World is established in a register so flatly naturalistic that singing becomes jarring
Heightening is inconsistent — some scenes feel like a musical, others like a straight drama
The emotional register drops sharply between numbers and never recovers before the next
signals_in_text: Non-musical scenes maintain a slightly elevated emotional pitch that anticipates the next number
Characters express desire, joy, and grief more directly than people typically do in life
Production or prose choices support a world where beauty and feeling are conspicuous

nodes.label: Musical Numbers Have Narrative Function
nodes.definition: Within the established world, every musical number is understood — by the story's logic and the audience — to serve a specific dramatic purpose: to reveal, to transform, to challenge, or to resolve. This is the structural analog of the heightened reality rule: just as the world accepts song as expression, the narrative architecture makes room for song as event. Numbers are not interruptions but nodes in the dramatic structure where the story's energy concentrates and changes direction.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
Story structure is being architected
exit_conditions: Each number is placed at a point of maximum dramatic necessity and serves a traceable narrative function
typical_variants: Desire number: establishes what the protagonist wants and why, typically early in act one
Complication number: a number that introduces or escalates a conflict or obstacle
Resolution or transformation number: a number that marks the end of one phase of the story
failure_modes: Numbers placed for variety or pacing without dramatic motivation
The story's main dramatic turns happen in book scenes, leaving numbers to comment rather than drive
The most dramatically charged moments are spoken rather than sung, inverting the genre's logic
signals_in_text: The song list reads as a sequence of dramatic events, not a playlist of moods
After each number, the story is in a different state than before it began
Characters who share a number emerge from it with a changed relationship or understanding

nodes.label: Ensemble Supports the Emotional Landscape
nodes.definition: In the musical, the ensemble — chorus, supporting players, community — is not mere background but an active participant in establishing and amplifying the emotional world. The ensemble reflects, contests, and magnifies the protagonist's interior state, functioning as a social body that gives scale and context to individual feeling. When a character's joy is shared by the ensemble, it becomes communal celebration; when they are isolated from the ensemble's movement or song, it dramatizes alienation. The ensemble's relationship to the central numbers is itself a storytelling tool.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
Community or ensemble characters are part of the story
exit_conditions: Ensemble participation in numbers is motivated by dramatic logic and contributes to the emotional meaning of each scene
typical_variants: Community chorus: ensemble represents a specific community whose relationship to the protagonist shapes the story
Counterpoint ensemble: ensemble voices complicate or contest the protagonist's emotional statement
Celebratory ensemble: ensemble amplifies the protagonist's feeling to the scale of shared human experience
failure_modes: Ensemble is present only as spectacle, with no relationship to the story's dramatic content
Ensemble numbers feel disconnected from the story world — a cast performing rather than a community feeling
Protagonist is isolated from the ensemble in ways that are never narratively meaningful
signals_in_text: Ensemble members have differentiated reactions to the protagonist's situation
Community numbers reveal something about the social world that shapes the protagonist's circumstances
The ensemble's energy in a scene indicates the social stakes of the moment

nodes.label: I Want Song
nodes.definition: Early in the story — typically within the first act — the protagonist must express through song what they want, need, or dream of in a way that speech has not yet been able to fully articulate. This number is foundational: it establishes the protagonist's desire as the engine of the story, gives the audience the emotional investment they need to follow the character through whatever comes next, and sets the thematic stakes of the entire piece. Without a clear I Want song, the audience has no compass for the protagonist's journey and no emotional framework for evaluating whether the story ultimately delivers what it promised.
entry_conditions: Protagonist is established in their current world
Desire is present but not yet fully expressed
exit_conditions: Audience understands clearly what the protagonist wants and why it matters to them deeply
typical_variants: Simple desire song: protagonist expresses a specific, concrete wish that drives the plot
Yearning song: protagonist expresses a diffuse but powerful longing for a different life
Dream song: protagonist articulates an imagined future that contrasts with their present reality
failure_modes: The protagonist's desire is stated in dialogue but never sung — the emotional commitment of the I Want form is avoided
The song expresses a desire so vague it gives the story no direction
The protagonist's stated desire in the I Want song has no relationship to what the story actually pursues
signals_in_text: Song occurs before the first act complication, establishing desire before obstacles arise
Lyrics move from the present state to an imagined or desired state
After the number, the audience could summarize what this character wants and why

nodes.label: Showstopper / Act-Break Number
nodes.definition: At the structural peaks of the story — typically the act-one finale and the climax — there must be a number of sufficient emotional and theatrical power to earn the break or the resolution. The act-break showstopper does not merely entertain; it crystallizes the central dramatic question at its most unresolved and sends the audience into the interval (or the story's final movement) with maximum investment. This number must be the largest emotional statement the show has made up to that point — anything smaller will feel like a structural failure, a story that has not yet found its gear.
entry_conditions: Dramatic tension has been built to a peak
The story is at a structural break or climax
exit_conditions: The central dramatic question is crystallized at maximum intensity
Audience is fully invested in the resolution
typical_variants: Confrontation showstopper: conflict between characters reaches its peak in a musically combative number
Revelation showstopper: a truth is revealed or a decision made that changes everything, expressed in a large number
Ensemble climax: the community comes together or fractures in a number that represents the story's turning point
failure_modes: Act break lands on a moment of low emotional intensity, leaving the audience disengaged at intermission
The showstopper is visually spectacular but emotionally empty — it impresses without involving
The climactic number is smaller or less emotionally decisive than numbers earlier in the show
signals_in_text: The number builds continuously from its opening to a peak that cannot be exceeded in that moment
After the number, the story cannot continue in the same way — something has broken open
The theatrical or cinematic resources are at their maximum in this number

nodes.label: Reprise or Transformation Number
nodes.definition: Late in the story, a number from earlier returns in transformed form — same melody, altered meaning — to show how the character or situation has changed. The reprise is one of musical theater's most powerful tools precisely because it leverages what the audience already knows: the original number carries its full emotional history into the new context, and the contrast between the two hearings does work that no new song can replicate. When a reprise works, it proves that the story has genuinely changed something; when it fails, it is merely a reminder that this song exists.
entry_conditions: An earlier number established a melody and emotional context that the story will need to revisit
Character or situation has changed significantly
exit_conditions: The return of the familiar melody in a new emotional context demonstrates the story's transformation
typical_variants: Ironic reprise: the original's optimism or desire is revisited in a context that reveals its cost or failure
Triumphant reprise: the original's aspiration is revisited at the moment of its fulfillment
Broken reprise: the melody is attempted but cannot be completed, dramatizing loss or defeat
failure_modes: Reprise is placed without dramatic motivation — the song returns because it was popular, not because the story needs it
The reprise lyrics are identical to the original, missing the opportunity to show transformation
Reprise arrives before the story has changed enough to warrant the return
signals_in_text: The melody of the original is recognizable but harmonically or rhythmically altered to signal transformation
Lyric changes, however small, carry the weight of everything that has happened since the original
The character's relationship to the song has visibly changed — they sing it differently now

nodes.label: Heightened, Emotionally Expansive, Performative Tone
nodes.definition: The musical operates in a tone that is simultaneously more emotionally exposed than naturalistic drama and more self-aware about the performative nature of that exposure. Characters in musicals feel things at full volume and express those feelings with a directness — through body, voice, and movement — that ordinary social life suppresses. This tone is not ironic about its own intensity; it holds the performative quality and the genuine emotional investment together without embarrassment, trusting the audience to accept that largeness of feeling is not the same as falseness of feeling.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Tone is maintained consistently through all elements: script, performance, design, and direction
typical_variants: Grandly theatrical: tone embraces spectacle and artifice as expressions of emotional truth
Intimately intense: tone is emotionally expansive but physically contained, making every gesture count
Comedically elevated: tone maintains emotional investment while permitting and amplifying comedy
failure_modes: Tone undercuts the emotional investment by winking at the audience about the artificiality of song
Tone is so earnest that comedy cannot coexist with it, making the show feel airless
Performance style is naturalistic in a way that makes the singing feel like a different, incompatible show
signals_in_text: Non-musical scenes maintain an elevated emotional register that anticipates song
Characters express internal states directly and fully rather than deflecting or underplaying
The style signals that the form's conventions are to be inhabited, not managed from a safe ironic distance

nodes.label: Songs That Halt Narrative Momentum
nodes.definition: The defining failure mode of the musical is numbers that stop the story rather than advancing it — where the audience recognizes that the plot has paused for entertainment and begins to wait for the next scene. This failure occurs when songs are placed for variety or spectacle rather than dramatic necessity, when they repeat in lyric form what dialogue has already established, or when they function as intermissions from the drama rather than its intensification. A show plagued by narrative-halting numbers trains the audience to disengage during songs, which is the catastrophic inversion of the musical form's fundamental promise.
entry_conditions: Musical numbers occur in the story
exit_conditions: Each number is verified to advance plot, reveal character, or change a relationship — not merely to entertain
typical_variants: The obligatory novelty number: a song placed for variety that no one in the show needs
The expository song: a number that tells the audience what they already know from the book scene
The production number trap: spectacle so lavish it overwhelms dramatic purpose
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a genre violation
Show is otherwise well-constructed but one or more numbers are identifiable halts
signals_in_text: The story's situation is identical before and after the number
Audiences skip or fast-forward this number on repeated viewings
The number could be lifted from the show and placed in a different show without adjustment

nodes.label: Inconsistent Rules for Why Characters Sing
nodes.definition: When a musical fails to establish or maintain a coherent internal logic for when and why characters sing, the audience loses the thread of the genre contract and every subsequent number becomes a small act of trust-breaking. This failure is especially destructive because it attacks the form's foundational premise: if the world does not have consistent rules about song, the audience can never settle into the suspension of disbelief that makes musical expression feel natural rather than arbitrary. The result is a show where some moments of song feel earned and others feel imposed, and the audience is perpetually uncertain which experience they are having.
entry_conditions: Multiple numbers have occurred with different or contradictory motivations for singing
exit_conditions: Internal song logic is established early and held throughout the piece
typical_variants: Mode shift without motivation: show begins as frank musical theater then attempts naturalistic scenes in which singing feels anachronistic
Selective singing: some characters in emotional distress sing and others in equivalent distress do not, with no logic explaining the difference
Diegetic confusion: the show is unclear whether characters hear each other's songs or only the audience does
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode — its presence indicates a foundational genre violation
Even individual numbers may be strong, but the show as a whole lacks coherent musical identity
signals_in_text: Audience members report confusion about which scenes are musical and which are not
Characters sometimes respond to each other's songs as events and sometimes do not
The emotional register required to motivate singing shifts between numbers without dramatic cause

edges.label: Promise requires songs that work dramatically
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every number must be accountable to the story's dramatic stakes, not just its entertainment value
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must be revealed or transformed through the act of singing, not merely displayed
common_alternatives: Some musicals foreground spectacle in production numbers while maintaining the constraint for protagonist numbers
anti_patterns: Treating songs as rewards for the audience rather than as events in the story

edges.label: Promise requires emotion to justify song
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes must be built into scenes leading to numbers so that the musical break feels like release rather than intrusion
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must have an emotional state at the moment of singing that speech cannot adequately express
common_alternatives: Comedy numbers may use surprise escalation — the absurdity of the situation rather than emotional intensity justifies the song
anti_patterns: Characters begin singing with no buildup, making song feel mechanical

edges.label: Promise requires a world with consistent song logic
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The plausibility of the entire form depends on the world's acceptance of song as natural expression
edges.effects_on_character: All characters must operate within the same musical logic — who can sing, when, and how
common_alternatives: Some musicals deliberately violate their own logic as a thematic device — but the logic must be established before it is broken
anti_patterns: Musical convention applied inconsistently, undermining audience trust in the form

edges.label: Promise requires unified musical and dramatic architecture
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The story's dramatic peaks and musical peaks must coincide for the form to function
edges.effects_on_character: Character arcs must be designed with knowledge of where the numbers fall
common_alternatives: Concept musicals may use deliberate disjunction between musical and narrative structure as a formal statement
anti_patterns: Story structure and song placement designed independently then combined, producing misalignment

edges.label: Promise requires heightened performative tone
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals that the world operates at a register where song is possible
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must be capable of the emotional largeness that singing requires
common_alternatives: Musical comedies modulate tone toward wit while maintaining emotional investment
anti_patterns: Naturalistic tone that makes song feel incongruous every time it occurs

edges.label: Songs-advance constraint realized in book musical form
preconditions: Story has a clear dramatic through-line with defined characters and spoken dialogue
edges.effects_on_stakes: Both book scenes and numbers carry the plot — neither is filler
edges.effects_on_character: Character consistency must be maintained across spoken and sung modes
common_alternatives: Rock opera
Musical comedy
Jukebox musical
anti_patterns: Book scenes treated as pauses between the real show, which is the songs

edges.label: Pre-existing songs require a world that makes them dramatically native
preconditions: Pre-existing song catalog is the musical foundation
Narrative must justify song placement through dramatic logic rather than biographical or chronological order
edges.effects_on_stakes: Story stakes must be strong enough to make familiar songs feel newly necessary
edges.effects_on_character: Characters must inhabit known songs as personal expression, not as performance of the original
common_alternatives: Book musical with original score
Rock opera
anti_patterns: Placing songs in their original release order rather than dramatic order

edges.label: Total musical-narrative integration in sung-through form
preconditions: Story and music are being designed as a single integrated artifact with no spoken dialogue
edges.effects_on_stakes: Music must carry all dramatic information — no spoken fallback for exposition or character clarification
edges.effects_on_character: Every aspect of character must be expressible in lyric and melody
common_alternatives: Book musical
Musical comedy
anti_patterns: Sung-through form that inserts spoken dialogue whenever the music cannot carry the drama

edges.label: Comic escalation justifies musical breaks
preconditions: Primary mode is comedic
Escalation in comedy is driven by absurdity and complication, not raw emotional intensity
edges.effects_on_stakes: Comic stakes are real but the audience trusts they will resolve happily
edges.effects_on_character: Characters are distinguished by their comic relationships to the world's complications
common_alternatives: Book musical
Jukebox musical
anti_patterns: Comedy that never uses the musical form's specific comic possibilities — surprise rhyme, tonal mismatch, physical comedy amplified by rhythm

edges.label: Book musical requires consistently heightened world
preconditions: Book musical subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Heightened reality makes the transition from book scene to song feel continuous rather than jarring
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' emotional fullness in spoken scenes anticipates and prepares for their sung fullness
common_alternatives: Some contemporary book musicals deliberately reduce heightening in book scenes to create contrast with the numbers
anti_patterns: Book scenes so naturalistic they set an emotional baseline that song cannot plausibly arise from

edges.label: Jukebox musical must give pre-existing songs dramatic function
preconditions: Jukebox subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience's prior knowledge of songs creates expectations that the dramatic context must either satisfy or productively subvert
edges.effects_on_character: Characters claiming familiar songs as personal expression requires dramatic motivation
common_alternatives: When biographical logic and dramatic logic align, placement can serve both
anti_patterns: Placing a song because it is popular or chronologically appropriate rather than because the story needs it here

edges.label: Sung-through form depends on ensemble to sustain emotional world
preconditions: Rock opera/sung-through subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Without spoken scenes, ensemble must carry exposition and context through musical participation
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist is defined partly through contrast with and support from the ensemble's musical voice
common_alternatives: Some sung-through works are essentially solo pieces with minimal ensemble presence
anti_patterns: Ensemble in a sung-through show that functions as backing track rather than as dramatized community

edges.label: Musical comedy operates in comedically elevated reality
preconditions: Musical comedy subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Comic heightening makes implausible situations feel native rather than arbitrary
edges.effects_on_character: Characters in musical comedy can behave in ways that would read as psychologically bizarre in a realistic world
common_alternatives: Some musical comedies set in more naturalistic worlds use the contrast between realistic setting and musical expression as a comic device
anti_patterns: Comic register so broad that genuine emotional investment becomes impossible

edges.label: Heightened world enables and requires the I Want declaration
preconditions: Heightened reality established
Protagonist has been introduced in their current world
edges.effects_on_stakes: The I Want song converts abstract desire into the story's central dramatic question
edges.effects_on_character: Protagonist's full emotional truth is expressed directly and completely for the first time
common_alternatives: Some musicals delay or subvert the I Want song but must substitute equivalent desire-establishment through other musical means
anti_patterns: Protagonist's desire established through dialogue alone, missing the form's most powerful tool for emotional investment

edges.label: Numbers with dramatic function build to the showstopper
preconditions: Multiple functionally-grounded numbers have built dramatic momentum
Story is at a structural peak
edges.effects_on_stakes: Showstopper must represent the maximum accumulation of dramatic stakes to that point
edges.effects_on_character: Character's situation must be at its most unresolved and charged in the showstopper
common_alternatives: Some shows use the act-break as a quiet devastating moment rather than a large showstopper, but must compensate with accumulated prior intensity
anti_patterns: Showstopper placed at a moment of low dramatic stakes — visually spectacular but emotionally arbitrary

edges.label: Ensemble emotional world frames the meaning of reprises
preconditions: Original number established a melody and emotional context
Story has transformed significantly since the original
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reprise demonstrates whether the story's transformation is real or cosmetic
edges.effects_on_character: Character's changed relationship to the original melody proves internal transformation
common_alternatives: Reprise may involve ensemble responding differently to a melody they shared in the original, showing collective transformation
anti_patterns: Reprise is identical to original in tone, losing the opportunity to demonstrate change

edges.label: Stated desire builds to act-break crisis
preconditions: I Want song established the protagonist's desire
Story has complicated and tested that desire
edges.effects_on_stakes: Showstopper must directly engage with the desire stated in the I Want song — its achievement, obstruction, or transformation
edges.effects_on_character: Character is at maximum distance from or proximity to their stated desire at the showstopper
common_alternatives: The showstopper may reveal the desire was wrong or insufficient, setting up act two's redefinition
anti_patterns: Showstopper has no relationship to the desire established in the I Want song

edges.label: Act-break crisis sets up transformation reprise
preconditions: Showstopper has crystallized the central dramatic question at maximum intensity
Story has moved through its final phase
edges.effects_on_stakes: Reprise payoff must feel proportionate to the emotional investment of the showstopper
edges.effects_on_character: Character's transformation from showstopper to reprise is the arc of act two in compressed form
common_alternatives: Broken reprise — the transformation fails and the melody cannot be completed — may serve as tragic resolution
anti_patterns: Reprise arrives too soon after the showstopper, before the story has done the work the transformation requires

edges.label: Songs that fail to advance become narrative halts
preconditions: A number has been placed without traceable dramatic function
edges.effects_on_stakes: Momentum is lost and the audience's investment in the story dissipates during the number
edges.effects_on_character: Characters appear to pause their dramatic arc to perform
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Failure to establish song logic produces inconsistency
preconditions: Song logic was not clearly established early
Or established logic has been violated without dramatic justification
edges.effects_on_stakes: Every subsequent number is experienced with a degree of uncertainty rather than settled acceptance
edges.effects_on_character: Characters' singing feels like authorial imposition rather than expressive necessity
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern — it describes what to avoid
